courseMetadata.title: Advanced React Architecture
courseMetadata.description: A production-grade course for professional consultants serving small-to-midsize SaaS companies. Treats React as a design system — a set of composable primitives, data-flow contracts, and trade-off surfaces — rather than a collection of APIs. Covers rendering mechanics, async UI coordination, TypeScript contract design, functional programming patterns, state topology, component design, forms, library selection, testing philosophy, performance architecture, and the integrative skill of prescribing and defending a React architecture for a real SaaS context.

prerequisites.description: At least 2 years of professional React development experience, including hooks (useState, useEffect, useContext, useReducer) and basic component composition.
Working knowledge of TypeScript: interfaces, generics at a basic level, and typed React props.
Familiarity with REST API consumption from a React front end (fetch, Axios, or similar).
Basic understanding of modern JavaScript build tooling (npm, bundlers, ES modules).
Prior Knowledge Check passed at >= 40% — learners scoring below this should complete an intermediate React course first.

interleavingGroups.rationaleNote: Rendering mechanics and async boundary design are tightly coupled: Suspense integrates directly with the scheduler; useTransition changes rendering priority. Interleaving these two competencies reinforces why concurrent features exist and how they affect component design.

TypeScript discriminated unions map directly to pure function outputs; generic hooks are higher-order functions at the type level. Interleaving these two reinforces that type safety and functional purity are complementary architectural disciplines.
State ownership decisions determine component boundaries. Interleaving state topology and component design prevents the common mistake of designing a component hierarchy before resolving data flow.

Form architecture decisions (RHF, Zod) are library decisions with architectural consequences. Interleaving these two reinforces that library selection is driven by state classification, not familiarity.

Testing strategy and performance architecture are both retrofitting-resistant: both are expensive to add after the fact. Interleaving reinforces that both are first-class design concerns, not finishing steps.
Section 11 is the terminal synthesis. All prior competencies are deliberately interleaved in audit and blueprint exercises to force integrative application across the full course vocabulary.

probeQuestions.prompt:
A parent component re-renders. Which of the following correctly describes what happens to its child components by default in React?

A) Children only re-render if their props changed.
B) Children re-render unconditionally unless wrapped in React.memo.
C) Children never re-render unless their own state changes.
D) Children re-render only if they use useEffect.
scoringCriteria: Learner understands that React re-renders child subtrees by default when a parent re-renders, regardless of prop changes

probeQuestions.prompt: You have a React component that fetches user data and renders it. Currently, loading and error states are managed with three separate boolean flags: isLoading, isError, and hasData. What is the primary architectural problem with this approach?
scoringCriteria: Identifies that three independent booleans allow logically impossible combinations (e.g. isLoading and isError both true simultaneously)
Mentions discriminated unions or mutually exclusive state as the correct alternative
Does not need to use exact terminology — identifying the contradiction class of bugs is sufficient

probeQuestions.prompt: A colleague says: 'We should put all our server-fetched data in a Redux store so it's available everywhere.' Identify one specific architectural problem with this approach and name the better mechanism for server state.
scoringCriteria: Identifies staleness or lack of background refetch as the problem with Redux for server state
Names TanStack Query, SWR, or a dedicated server cache layer as the alternative
Partial credit for identifying the wrong-layer problem without naming the alternative

probeQuestions.prompt: What is the difference between a Suspense boundary and an Error Boundary in React? When would you use each?
scoringCriteria: Correctly identifies Suspense as handling async loading states (components that suspend)
Correctly identifies Error Boundary as catching rendering errors
Mentions that they are typically composed together for full resilience

probeQuestions.prompt: A React component tree has a modal rendered inside a div with overflow: hidden applied. The modal appears clipped. What React feature resolves this, and why does it work?
scoringCriteria: Names React Portals (createPortal) as the solution
Explains that Portals render the DOM output into a different node, escaping the parent's CSS stacking context
Partial credit for naming Portals without explaining the stacking context mechanism

competencies.title: React Rendering Mechanics & the Virtual DOM Contract
competencies.description: Deep understanding of how React schedules, batches, and commits renders; the reconciliation algorithm; fiber architecture; and the rules that govern when components re-render.

desiredOutcomes.description: Explain the three phases of a React render cycle and what work happens in each.
Predict which components re-render given a specific state or context change and justify the prediction using reconciliation rules.
Distinguish concurrent rendering behavior from the legacy synchronous model and explain implications for component design.
Diagnose a component tree for unnecessary re-renders and identify the correct mitigation strategy.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Re-rendering means the real DOM is fully updated.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: React's reconciliation diffing means only changed nodes are committed to the DOM. Re-rendering is a JavaScript function call that produces a new element tree; actual DOM updates happen only in the commit phase after diffing.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Resurface the three-phase render diagram. Have the learner trace a single state update through render → reconcile → commit, identifying at which phase the DOM is touched. Follow with a worked example showing re-renders with zero DOM mutations.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: useEffect runs after every render.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: useEffect runs after renders where its dependency array values have changed by reference equality. An empty array means run once; a stable dependency means run only when that dependency changes.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a side-by-side comparison of useEffect with no array, empty array, and a dependency array. Have learner predict when each fires given a sequence of state changes. Provide elaborative feedback explaining reference equality for objects.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: React 18/19 concurrent features change what components do.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Concurrency changes when and how often React renders components — the scheduler behavior — not the component contract itself. Pure components written for React 17 work correctly in React 18+ without modification.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a concrete before/after showing the same component code running in synchronous and concurrent mode. Ask the learner to identify what changed in the component — answer: nothing. Reinforce that the change is in the scheduler, not the component.

competencies.title: Async UI Architecture: Suspense, Error Boundaries & Concurrent Features
competencies.description: Designing async UI coordination using Suspense boundaries and Error Boundaries as architectural placement decisions; using useTransition, useDeferredValue, and the use() hook (React 19) to keep UIs responsive during data loading and state transitions.

desiredOutcomes.description: Explain the architectural role of Suspense as a declarative async boundary system, distinguishing it from the narrow React.lazy use case.
Design a Suspense boundary layout for a SaaS feature with multiple independent async data regions, justifying granularity decisions.
Apply useTransition and useDeferredValue correctly and explain when each is preferable.
Demonstrate use of the React 19 use() hook and explain its relationship to Suspense.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Suspense is for lazy-loading components only.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: React.lazy is one use case. Architecturally, Suspense is a boundary system for coordinating async states across a component tree — replacing scattered loading booleans with a declarative fallback contract. Most developers have only seen the React.lazy pattern.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a before/after: a component with isLoading/isError/data flags vs. the same component Suspense-enabled with a boundary. Ask the learner to list what the Suspense version eliminates. Follow with a boundary placement exercise.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Error Boundaries are a last-resort catch-all at the app root.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Boundary granularity is a design decision. A single root-level boundary means any render error crashes the entire application. Scoped boundaries co-located with the features they protect limit blast radius.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a scenario where a billing widget crash crashes the whole dashboard. Ask the learner where they would add boundaries to prevent this. Provide elaborative feedback on the granularity principle and its UX consequences.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: useTransition is a performance micro-optimization.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: useTransition is an architectural tool for classifying UI updates as urgent vs. non-urgent. This classification changes how React prioritises rendering across the whole update pipeline, not just one component.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a search input example without and with useTransition. Have the learner predict what the user experiences in each case. Reinforce that the architectural decision is about intent classification — urgent vs. non-urgent — not about millisecond optimization.

competencies.title: TypeScript as an Architectural Contract Layer
competencies.description: Using TypeScript generics, discriminated unions, branded types, and strict prop/state typing to encode architectural constraints and invariants at the type level rather than at runtime.

desiredOutcomes.description: Define discriminated union types that model all valid states of a UI feature.
Write generic React components and custom hooks that preserve full type inference at the call site.
Evaluate prop types and identify where any, missing narrowing, or loose types create architectural contract holes.
Apply branded/nominal types to distinguish domain entities sharing the same primitive representation.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: TypeScript in React is just typed props.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Effective TypeScript in React encodes state machine shapes, event bus schemas, and async lifecycle contracts. Type-only-at-the-boundary usage defeats most of its architectural value as a constraint enforcement tool.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a hook with any return type vs. one with a discriminated union return. Have learner identify what bugs the typed version prevents. Elaborate on the difference between typing the boundary vs. typing the interior.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: any is a pragmatic escape hatch.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: any silently disables the contract for all downstream consumers of that type, creating invisible architectural holes. unknown with narrowing is the correct pragmatic alternative — it forces the developer to verify shape before use.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Trace an any type from an API boundary through five consuming components. Show which bugs become invisible. Then replace with unknown and show the narrowing required at the trust boundary. Have learner identify what each approach requires of the developer.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Generics are an advanced feature for library authors only.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Generic components and hooks are the primary tool for building reusable, type-safe abstractions in application code. A typed list component, a useAsync hook, and a paginated table all require generics to preserve type inference at the call site.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a non-generic List component that returns any[] at the call site, then the generic version. Have learner write a call site for each and observe what TypeScript knows about the item type. Reinforce that generics enable type-safe reuse, not just library APIs.

competencies.title: Functional Programming Principles in React
competencies.description: Understanding and applying immutability, pure functions, function composition, higher-order functions, and declarative data transformation as they manifest in React architecture.

desiredOutcomes.description: Distinguish pure from impure functions and explain why component render functions must be pure.
Apply function composition and higher-order functions to build reusable data transformation pipelines.
Explain structural sharing and demonstrate how Immer achieves immutability without full cloning.
Compose custom React hooks from smaller hooks and explain what distinguishes a well-designed hook contract from mere code extraction.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: React is functional programming.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: React uses FP idioms but does not enforce referential transparency or purity. Side effects are explicitly managed via useEffect and mutations inside reducers via Immer, rather than eliminated. React is FP-influenced, not FP-strict.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a correct React component with a useEffect (a side effect) and ask the learner whether this is 'functional programming'. Walk through the distinction between FP influence (pure render, composition) and FP purity (no side effects ever).

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Immutability means cloning everything.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Structural sharing achieves immutability's benefits without full object cloning. Immer and Zustand use proxy-based structural sharing — only the changed path creates new references; unchanged branches are shared with the previous version.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Draw the structural sharing diagram: a tree where one leaf changes and only that path gets new nodes. Show Immer's produce output. Have learner predict which references are new and which are shared after a single field update.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Function composition is a utility function technique, not an architectural pattern.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: In React architecture, composition manifests at the component level (wrapping, children patterns), the hook level (composing custom hooks from smaller hooks), and in data pipelines (selector composition in Redux/Reselect). It is a structural principle, not a utility trick.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show three examples of composition at three levels: a HOC wrapping a component, a custom hook composing three smaller hooks, and a Reselect selector composing two input selectors. Ask the learner to identify the common pattern. Reinforce that the same principle operates at all levels.

competencies.title: State Topology & Ownership Design
competencies.description: Ability to classify state by kind (server, client-UI, derived, global, ephemeral, form), assign ownership correctly, and select the right mechanism for each — including the architectural limits of the Context API as a dependency injection tool rather than a state manager.

desiredOutcomes.description: Classify a given set of application state variables by kind: server cache, global client, local UI, derived, ephemeral, form.
Design a state ownership map for a realistic SaaS feature.
Explain the architectural consequences of placing server state in a client global store versus a dedicated server-cache layer.
Implement the Context value/dispatch separation pattern and explain why Context is a DI mechanism, not a state manager.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: All shared state belongs in a global store.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Global stores are appropriate for truly cross-cutting state. Lifting state to the nearest common ancestor or using composition is often simpler and more maintainable. Over-globalising state creates unnecessary coupling and re-render surface.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a feature with three pieces of state: one local (dropdown open), one feature-scoped (filter selection), one cross-cutting (user session). Have learner classify each and select the mechanism. Elaborate on why each classification leads to a different choice.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Context is a lightweight state manager.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Context is a dependency injection mechanism. Every consumer re-renders when the context value changes by reference, making naive context use a performance liability at scale. The correct pattern separates value from dispatch and uses multiple focused contexts.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a large AppContext with 5 values. Have learner trace which components re-render when one value changes. Then show the split Context pattern and repeat the trace. The contrast makes the performance consequence visible.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Server state and client state are the same thing.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Server state has fundamentally different lifecycle characteristics: it goes stale (other users change it), needs background refetch, requires cache invalidation, and deduplicates concurrent requests. Client state has none of these concerns.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a side-by-side table of server state properties vs. client state properties. Then show the code required to manually implement staleness, background refetch, and invalidation in Redux. Ask: what does TanStack Query eliminate from this list?

competencies.title: Component Design Patterns & Composition Models
competencies.description: Mastery of compound components, render props, higher-order components, the container/presentational split, headless components, Portals, and custom hook design as an abstraction boundary — knowing when each applies and why, including accessibility implications.

desiredOutcomes.description: Implement a compound component pattern for a UI widget requiring flexible internal composition.
Build a headless component separating behavior from visual presentation and explain its testability and accessibility advantages.
Evaluate a component design and select the most appropriate pattern, defending against alternatives.
Implement a React Portal and explain why placement matters for stacking context, focus management, and accessibility.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Hooks replaced all older component patterns.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Hooks replaced some use cases for HOCs and render props, but compound components and headless patterns solve fundamentally different problems — composition flexibility and behavioral/visual separation — that hooks alone do not address.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a tabs widget requirement. Ask learner to implement it with hooks alone. Then show the compound component version. Ask: which gives the consumer layout flexibility? Identify what the compound pattern provides that the hook alone cannot.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: More abstraction is always better architecture.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Premature abstraction with compound components or HOCs creates indirection that increases cognitive load without adding flexibility. The correct pattern is the simplest one that meets the constraint.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a compound component built for a widget whose layout never changes. Have learner count the number of files, exports, and concepts introduced. Ask: what did this abstraction gain? Contrast with a simple props-based version that meets the same requirements.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Custom hooks are just extracted code.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A well-designed custom hook encapsulates a complete behavioral contract — thinkable as a unit, testable in isolation, composable without knowledge of its internals. Hooks that merely extract lines of code without defining a clear boundary create leaky abstractions.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Apply the three-question contract test to a hook that is merely extracted code. Show how it fails all three questions. Then refactor it to a proper contract and show how all three questions pass. Have learner write a renderHook test for each version.

competencies.title: Forms Architecture: State, Validation, and Mutation Design
competencies.description: Designing form state as a distinct architectural concern; selecting between controlled and uncontrolled form strategies; integrating React Hook Form with Zod for type-safe validation; and understanding how form submission connects to server mutation patterns.

desiredOutcomes.description: Explain the trade-offs between fully controlled forms and React Hook Form's register pattern in terms of render performance and coupling.
Implement a React Hook Form + Zod schema generating TypeScript types and runtime validators from a single source of truth.
Design the state lifecycle of a form submission — from ephemeral input through validation to async mutation state — mapping each phase to the correct React mechanism.
Classify form state in a topology map and explain why it is distinct from general client UI state and server cache state.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Forms are just controlled components with useState.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Fully controlled forms re-render on every keystroke, coupling validation logic tightly to component state. For complex forms, uncontrolled strategies (React Hook Form's register pattern) decouple render frequency from input events — both a performance and architecture advantage.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a 10-field controlled form. Have learner count re-renders per keystroke using React DevTools. Then show the RHF version. Compare re-render counts. Ask: for a 3-field form, does this matter? For a 20-field form with expensive validation?

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Validation is a UI concern only.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Validation logic expressed only in the UI layer cannot be shared with API request validation or server-side logic. Zod schemas serve as a single source of truth generating both TypeScript types and runtime validators, bridging front-end and back-end contracts.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a scenario where the UI validates email format but the API uses a different rule. Walk through the bug that results. Then show a Zod schema exported to both layers. Have learner identify what changed and what was eliminated.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Form state is just another kind of client state.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Form state has a unique lifecycle: ephemeral until submission, with field-level and form-level validity states, coordinating with async mutation state (pending, error, success). Conflating it with general client state leads to over-complex stores and under-designed UX.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Map the form state lifecycle on a timeline: first keystroke → validation trigger → submission → pending → success/error → reset. Ask learner to identify which phases a general client state manager handles natively vs. which they would have to implement manually. Contrast with RHF.

competencies.title: Performance Architecture: Code Splitting, Bundling & Render Optimization
competencies.description: Designing performance into the application architecture through code splitting strategy, lazy loading placement, bundle analysis, virtualization for large data sets, and understanding how the React Compiler changes the memoization calculus.

desiredOutcomes.description: Design a code splitting strategy for a SaaS application, identifying route-level and component-level split points and their architectural rationale.
Use bundle analysis to identify oversized chunks and explain the architectural choices that caused them.
Apply list virtualization and articulate when it is architecturally necessary vs. premature.
Explain the React Compiler's automatic memoization model and identify which useMemo/useCallback patterns become redundant.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: useMemo and useCallback are always safe to add speculatively.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: These hooks have their own cost: memory overhead and a comparison on every render. Adding them speculatively rather than in response to a measured problem is premature optimization that adds noise. With the React Compiler, many manual memoizations are redundant.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a component with 10 unnecessary useMemo calls on cheap computations. Walk through the overhead: one comparison per render per memo. Then run the React DevTools profiler on both versions. Ask: which is faster for computations under 1ms?

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Code splitting is a build-time concern, not an architecture concern.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Where you place split boundaries determines which code ships to which users and when. Route-level splitting is a default; component-level splitting for heavy features requires deliberate architectural placement decisions that affect the component tree structure.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a 1.8MB bundle where pdfjs-dist is included in the main chunk. Ask: which users are paying for this? Then show the split version where it loads on demand. Reinforce that the split boundary decision determines user-facing load time — an architectural, not build, decision.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Performance optimization is done after the app is built.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Architectural decisions made early — component granularity, import discipline, rendering model choices — are far cheaper to correct than retrofitting code splitting or virtualization into a deeply coupled component tree.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a deeply coupled component tree where adding code splitting would require extracting and rewriting 8 components. Contrast with a loosely coupled tree where a single lazy() call suffices. Ask: which architecture was cheaper to make fast?

competencies.title: React Library Ecosystem Selection & Integration
competencies.description: Ability to evaluate, select, and correctly integrate Redux Toolkit, Zustand, TanStack Query, React Router, and build tooling (Vite, framework selection) — with explicit awareness of trade-offs and criteria for choosing between them.

desiredOutcomes.description: Compare Redux Toolkit and Zustand across complexity, boilerplate, middleware, and team scalability, and select the appropriate tool for a given SaaS context.
Configure TanStack Query for server cache management including cache invalidation strategy and optimistic update patterns.
Design a routing architecture using React Router supporting RBAC, code splitting, and nested layouts.
Evaluate the build tooling decision (Vite vs. framework selection) for an SMB SaaS project.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Redux Toolkit and Zustand solve the same problem.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: RTK is optimized for complex, normalized, event-sourced state with middleware; Zustand is optimized for simple, low-boilerplate client state. They target different parts of the state topology and are not interchangeable in most real-world SaaS applications.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present two feature requirements: a notification queue and an audit-log-driven order state machine. Have learner select a tool for each and justify. Elaborate on why the characteristics of each requirement match different tools.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: TanStack Query replaces your state manager.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: TanStack Query manages server cache state. It does not manage client-UI state or derived cross-cutting state. Using it to replace a global store conflates two distinct state kinds and leaves client state without a home.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: List five state variables from a real SaaS app. Have learner attempt to model each in TanStack Query. For the three that are client-only (sidebar open, selected theme, notification queue), show why there is no sensible queryFn to write. Reinforce: TanStack Query is for data that has a server.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Adding a library is an additive, reversible decision.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Libraries introduce bundle size, API surface dependency, and team cognitive load that are difficult to reverse. Architectural library choices have long-term maintenance consequences that compound as the codebase grows.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a real case: a project that adopted MobX early, then needed to migrate away 18 months later. Walk through what had to change: every component that used observer(), every store reference, every test mock. Ask: what was the true cost of this 'additive' decision?

competencies.title: Testing Architecture for React Applications
competencies.description: Designing a testing strategy appropriate for a SaaS front end; writing tests that encode architectural contracts; using Jest, React Testing Library, renderHook, and MSW correctly.

desiredOutcomes.description: Design a three-layer testing strategy appropriate for a given SaaS front end's risk profile.
Write React Testing Library integration tests verifying component behavior from a user interaction perspective, avoiding implementation detail coupling.
Configure MSW and explain why it is architecturally superior to mocking fetch or Axios directly.
Evaluate an existing test suite and identify brittle, redundant, or low-value tests.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: High test coverage means high test quality.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Coverage measures what code is executed by tests, not whether tests verify meaningful behavior or catch regressions. A suite of trivial snapshot tests can achieve 90% coverage while providing little safety against real user-facing bugs.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a test suite with 90% coverage composed entirely of snapshot tests. Present a behavioral regression (a button that no longer works). Ask: does any test catch this? Then show an RTL integration test that would. Contrast what each approach actually verifies.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Testing implementation details produces more thorough tests.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Testing internal state, private methods, or DOM node structures couples tests to implementation, causing them to break on correct refactors. Tests should verify behavior from the user's or consumer's perspective.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a test that asserts on component internal state. Then perform a correct refactor (useState → useReducer). The test breaks. Show an RTL test for the same behavior. Repeat the refactor. The RTL test passes. Ask: which test was right?

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Unit tests are always preferable to integration tests.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: For React components, integration tests (rendering a subtree with realistic data) typically provide higher value per maintenance cost than heavily mocked unit tests. Heavy mocking leaves so little real code under test that the value rarely justifies the cost.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a component unit test with 6 mocked dependencies. Ask: what real code is being tested here? Then show an RTL integration test with MSW for the same component. Ask: what real code is being tested now? Have learner rank value-to-maintenance cost for each.

competencies.title: Architectural Prescription & Consultative Communication
competencies.description: Ability to audit an existing React codebase, identify architectural liabilities, prescribe a coherent architecture for a SaaS context, and communicate trade-offs to both technical and non-technical stakeholders.

desiredOutcomes.description: Conduct a structured architectural audit and produce a prioritised list of liabilities with remediation recommendations.
Design a complete architectural blueprint (state topology, component model, async boundary map, library stack, testing strategy) from a product requirements brief.
Justify architectural decisions in business trade-off terms for a non-technical SaaS stakeholder audience.
Evaluate two competing architectural proposals and produce a written recommendation with explicit, defensible criteria.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The best architecture is the most technically sophisticated one.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: For SMB SaaS contexts, the best architecture is the one the team can maintain and extend given their size, skill level, and time horizon. Sophistication has a carrying cost that must be justified by actual requirements.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present RTK + Sagas for a 4-person SaaS team building a notification center. Have learner estimate the onboarding cost, the lines of boilerplate, and the maintenance burden. Then show Zustand for the same feature. Ask: which is better for this team?

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Architecture decisions are made once at project start.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Architecture is an ongoing practice. SMB SaaS products change faster than enterprise products; architecture must be revisited as product shape evolves. Good architectural recommendations include explicit review triggers.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show an architectural decision made at project start (Zustand over RTK) becoming the wrong choice 12 months later when an audit log requirement arrives. Ask: what would have made this decision correctly revisitable? Introduce the review trigger concept.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Non-technical stakeholders don't need to understand architectural trade-offs.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: SMB SaaS clients make staffing, timeline, and scope decisions that are directly shaped by architectural constraints. Communicating trade-offs in business terms is a core consulting skill — not optional communication overhead.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present an architectural decision (TanStack Query migration) expressed in technical terms vs. business terms. Have learner role-play as a product owner and identify which version enables them to make a budget decision. Elaborate on the translation principle.

questions.prompt: A Modal component is wrapped in React.memo. Its parent re-renders. The Modal's props are identical to the previous render. What happens, and why?
successCriteria: States that React.memo performs a shallow prop comparison
Correctly concludes that Modal skips re-rendering (bails out) because props are unchanged
Does not claim the DOM is involved in this decision

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies the bailout, explains shallow prop comparison, and notes that the bail out is a JavaScript-level skip — the DOM is not touched.
rubric.adequate: Correctly states Modal skips re-rendering but does not explain the shallow comparison mechanism.
rubric.weak: Incorrectly states the Modal re-renders, or confuses React.memo with PureComponent in a way that changes the answer.

errorPatterns.description: Learner states that re-rendering always means a DOM update, or that React.memo prevents DOM updates rather than component function calls.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the three-phase render diagram from section s01. Explicitly distinguish the render phase (function call) from the commit phase (DOM mutation). Ask learner to identify at which phase React.memo acts.

questions.prompt: A useEffect has a dependency array of [user] where user is an object created inline: const user = { id: userId, name }. The effect fires on every render despite userId and name not changing. Explain the root cause.
successCriteria: Identifies that user is a new object reference on every render
Explains that useEffect uses reference equality for object dependencies
Proposes a correct fix: memoize the object, use primitive dependencies, or move the object outside the component

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies reference equality as the cause, explains that object literals are new references every render, and proposes a concrete fix with correct reasoning.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the reference equality issue but proposes an incomplete or partially incorrect fix.
rubric.weak: Attributes the issue to useEffect 'always running for objects' or proposes deep equality comparison as the primary fix without explaining the root cause.

errorPatterns.description: Learner believes useEffect uses deep equality for objects, or that it runs after every render regardless of dependencies.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show a concrete example: two objects with identical property values but different references failing ===. Then show the same primitives passing ===. Link this to how useEffect's dependency comparison works.

questions.prompt: A dashboard has 12 chart components that all re-render when a single filter changes. Profiling shows each chart renders in 1.5ms. Total re-render cost: 18ms. Should you add React.memo to all charts? Justify your answer with a performance reasoning model.
successCriteria: Calculates or estimates the memoization overhead vs. savings
Identifies 18ms as likely acceptable (within 16ms frame budget or marginal)
Notes that React.memo adds comparison overhead on every render
Recommends profiling first and memoizing only where measured benefit exceeds cost

rubric.excellent: Applies the measure-first principle, estimates the comparison overhead vs. savings, and recommends targeted memoization only where profiling shows net gain. Notes that speculative memo can be counterproductive.
rubric.adequate: Recommends profiling before memoizing but does not quantify the overhead vs. savings trade-off.
rubric.weak: Recommends adding React.memo to all charts without considering overhead, or refuses to memoize based on a rule rather than measurement.

errorPatterns.description: Learner recommends adding React.memo speculatively to all components as a safe default, without considering comparison overhead.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show profiler output with and without memo on cheap renders. Demonstrate cases where memo adds net cost. Reinforce: memo is a measured optimization, not a safety net.

questions.prompt: A SaaS dashboard has one Suspense boundary at the app root wrapping all routes. The revenue chart takes 2 seconds to load; the user header loads from cache in 50ms. What does the user see, and what is the architectural problem?
successCriteria: States that the user sees a full-page spinner for 2 seconds despite the header being ready
Identifies the problem as coarse boundary granularity blocking fast regions with slow ones
Proposes independent boundaries per region as the fix

rubric.excellent: Correctly describes the user experience, names coarse granularity as the architectural problem, and proposes scoped boundaries with correct reasoning about independence.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the problem as the single boundary but describes the fix imprecisely or incompletely.
rubric.weak: Accepts the described behavior as correct, or proposes a fix unrelated to boundary placement (e.g. faster API).

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats a single root-level Suspense boundary as correct or optimal, and does not recognize the granularity problem.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Suspense boundary granularity section. Have learner draw the component tree and mark which regions are independent. Ask: if you had separate boundaries, when would each region appear?

questions.prompt: An Error Boundary is placed at the app root. A TypeError thrown inside a useEffect callback in the InvoiceList component causes the entire application to go blank. Two questions: (a) Does the Error Boundary catch this error? (b) What boundary design would have prevented the app from going blank?
successCriteria: Correctly states that Error Boundaries do NOT catch errors in useEffect callbacks (only render-phase errors)
Proposes feature-scoped Error Boundaries around InvoiceList
Notes that the useEffect error requires separate handling (try-catch in the effect, or mutation error state)

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies the useEffect limitation, proposes scoped boundaries, and addresses the useEffect error with a correct alternative strategy.
rubric.adequate: Correctly identifies that Error Boundaries don't catch useEffect errors but does not provide a complete fix for both the boundary design and the useEffect error handling.
rubric.weak: Believes the Error Boundary should catch the useEffect error, or proposes only moving the boundary without addressing the useEffect limitation.

errorPatterns.description: Learner believes Error Boundaries catch all errors in their subtree, including useEffect and event handler errors.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the Error Boundary limitations list from section s02. Have learner categorize a list of error types as caught or not caught by Error Boundaries. Reinforce: only render-phase errors are caught.

questions.prompt: A search input updates a large filtered list. The list re-render takes 80ms. Without useTransition, describe the user experience. With useTransition wrapping the list state update, describe the experience. Then: when would you use useDeferredValue instead?
successCriteria: Without useTransition: input feels laggy because 80ms renders block keystroke processing
With useTransition: input updates immediately; list renders in background; isPending shows indicator
useDeferredValue: when you receive a value from props or a library and don't control the setter

rubric.excellent: Accurately describes both user experiences with mechanistic reasoning. Correctly identifies the setter-control distinction as the useTransition vs. useDeferredValue decision criterion.
rubric.adequate: Correctly describes useTransition behavior but conflates useTransition and useDeferredValue or is imprecise about the decision criterion.
rubric.weak: Describes useTransition as a debounce mechanism, or claims useDeferredValue is always preferable.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats useTransition as a debounce or throttle utility rather than a priority classification API.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the useTransition section. Have learner identify: what is marked urgent vs. non-urgent in the search example? Reinforce that the mechanism is priority classification, not timing control.

questions.prompt: A hook returns: { isLoading: boolean; isError: boolean; data?: User }. A consumer writes: if (!isLoading && !isError) { display(data!) }. What is the type system problem, and how does a discriminated union fix it?
successCriteria: Identifies that three boolean flags allow impossible combinations and that data can be undefined even when both flags are false
Explains that the non-null assertion data! suppresses a real potential runtime error
Shows or describes a discriminated union that makes data's presence conditional on status === 'success'

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies the impossible state problem, explains why the non-null assertion is dangerous, and provides or describes a correct discriminated union that eliminates the problem.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the boolean flag problem but does not fully explain the discriminated union solution.
rubric.weak: Accepts the code as correct, or proposes adding a fourth boolean hasData without recognizing the underlying type modeling problem.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes adding a hasData boolean or optional chaining rather than addressing the underlying type model.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show the full enumeration of possible flag combinations. Count the impossible ones. Then show the discriminated union and count the possible states: exactly the valid ones. Ask: which approach makes impossible states unrepresentable?

questions.prompt: A function is typed: function processApiResponse(data: any). A colleague says: 'any is fine here — it's just the API boundary.' Explain the architectural problem with any at this location and show the correct alternative.
successCriteria: Identifies that any propagates through all downstream consumers, silently disabling type checking
Explains that unknown forces narrowing at the trust boundary, keeping everything downstream safe
Shows or describes a type guard or Zod parse as the correct narrowing mechanism

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies downstream propagation of any, explains unknown as the alternative, and shows a concrete narrowing strategy (type guard or Zod).
rubric.adequate: Identifies the any problem and names unknown as the alternative but does not show a concrete narrowing approach.
rubric.weak: Defends any as pragmatic, or proposes TypeScript // @ts-ignore comments as an equivalent alternative.

errorPatterns.description: Learner defends any at API boundaries as pragmatic or equivalent to unknown.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Trace the any type from the API boundary into five consuming components. Show which type errors become invisible. Then replace with unknown and show the narrowing required. Ask: in which version does TypeScript catch a misspelled property name?

questions.prompt: A function signature is: function assignTask(taskId: string, userId: string). The function is called incorrectly as assignTask(userId, taskId) — swapped arguments — and TypeScript does not error. Design a type-level fix with zero runtime overhead.
successCriteria: Proposes branded types: type TaskId = string & { __brand: 'TaskId' }, type UserId = string & { __brand: 'UserId' }
Shows the updated function signature using branded types
Explains that the brand is a phantom type — no runtime representation, zero cost
Notes constructor functions at the trust boundary for creating branded values

rubric.excellent: Proposes correct branded type syntax, updates the signature, explains zero runtime overhead, and addresses the trust boundary construction pattern.
rubric.adequate: Proposes branded types with correct intent but imprecise syntax or omits the trust boundary constructor.
rubric.weak: Proposes runtime checks or enums as the solution, missing the compile-time constraint requirement.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes runtime validation or enums instead of phantom branded types, missing the zero-runtime-overhead requirement.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show the branded type definition and compiled JavaScript output — the brand disappears at compile time. Contrast with a runtime check that always executes. Reinforce: branded types are a type-system-only constraint.

questions.prompt: A component's render function increments a module-level counter on every call: renderCount++. In React 18 concurrent mode, this counter may reach values you don't expect. Why?
successCriteria: Identifies that React 18 may call the render function multiple times before committing (e.g. abandoned renders in concurrent mode)
Explains that side effects in render functions are impure and produce unexpected results
States that side effects belong in useEffect or event handlers

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies concurrent mode's potential for multiple render calls, explains impurity as the root cause, and correctly relocates the side effect.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the impurity problem but does not specifically reference concurrent mode's abandoned renders as the mechanism.
rubric.weak: Believes the counter will always match the number of committed renders.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats render function calls as equivalent to committed renders, missing the concurrent mode rerendering behavior.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the concurrent rendering section. Have learner trace what happens when React abandons an in-progress render. Count how many times the component function is called vs. committed. Reinforce: purity is required precisely because React may call render multiple times.

questions.prompt: A reducer mutates state in place: state.items.push(newItem); return state. A useSelector that depends on state.items doesn't re-run. Why, and how does Immer fix this at zero algorithm cost?
successCriteria: Identifies that mutating state.items in place does not change the state object's reference
Explains that useSelector uses reference equality to detect change — same reference means no update
Describes Immer's produce: mutation syntax on a draft, structurally shared immutable output with new references on the changed path

rubric.excellent: Correctly traces the reference equality failure, explains structural sharing, and accurately describes what Immer's produce outputs.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the reference equality issue and mentions Immer but does not explain structural sharing.
rubric.weak: Believes the selector should deep-compare state to detect the change.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes deep equality comparison as the fix, missing the immutability requirement.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show the performance cost of deep equality on a large state tree vs. reference equality. Then show Immer's structural sharing output. Ask: why is reference equality correct after Immer but not after direct mutation?

questions.prompt: Apply the three-question hook contract test to this hook: function useUserStuff(id) { const [user, setUser] = useState(null); /* 40 lines */ return { user, setUser, loading, setLoading }; }. Does it pass? What would a correct contract look like?
successCriteria: Applies all three questions: describability in one sentence, replaceable internals, testable without internal knowledge
Identifies that returning setUser and setLoading leaks internal state — fails question 3
Proposes a contract version that returns state and named action dispatchers only

rubric.excellent: Correctly applies all three questions, identifies the leaky return type as the failure, and proposes a concrete contract interface with named actions only.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the leaky return as problematic but applies the three questions incompletely.
rubric.weak: Accepts the hook as a valid contract because it works correctly at runtime.

errorPatterns.description: Learner equates 'the hook works' with 'the hook has a good contract', missing the encapsulation and testability requirements.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Have learner write a renderHook test for the hook as-is. Then refactor the hook internals (swap useState for useReducer). Show which tests break. Then do the same for the contract version. The contract version's tests survive.

questions.prompt: Classify each of the following state variables for a SaaS project management app: (1) list of projects from GET /api/projects, (2) whether the sidebar is expanded, (3) total project count, (4) draft text in a new project name field, (5) current user authentication session.
successCriteria: Project list: server cache (TanStack Query)
Sidebar expanded: local UI or global client (useState or Zustand)
Total project count: derived (useMemo — never stored)
Draft text: form state (React Hook Form or local useState)
Auth session: global client (Zustand or Context)

rubric.excellent: Correctly classifies all five with correct mechanism assignments and briefly justifies each classification.
rubric.adequate: Correctly classifies at least four of five. May misplace one (commonly: puts project count in global store, or puts auth session in Context without noting its DI role).
rubric.weak: Puts server state in Redux, stores derived state as useState, or treats form draft as global state.

errorPatterns.description: Learner places all state in a global store regardless of kind, or stores derived state with useState.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the state classification framework table. Have learner re-apply the six classification questions to each state variable in order. Elaborate: why is project count derived? What would happen if it got out of sync with the project list?

questions.prompt: A developer stores all server-fetched project data in a Redux slice. Three months later, users report seeing deleted projects until they hard-refresh. Explain the root cause using the server state vs. client state distinction, and prescribe the correct architectural change.
successCriteria: Identifies Redux as having no staleness or invalidation mechanism
Explains that server state cached in Redux never refreshes without explicit action dispatch
Prescribes TanStack Query with appropriate staleTime and invalidateQueries on mutation

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies the staleness mechanism gap, explains what TanStack Query provides automatically, and outlines a concrete migration approach.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the staleness problem and names TanStack Query but does not explain the specific mechanisms (staleTime, invalidateQueries) that solve it.
rubric.weak: Proposes polling at a fixed interval as the fix, missing the architectural misclassification as the root cause.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes manual polling or a refetch action rather than addressing the misclassification of server state as client state.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show the properties table: server state lifecycle vs. client state lifecycle. Ask learner to check each property against what Redux provides natively. For each gap, ask: what code would you have to write to fill this? Then show TanStack Query handling all of them automatically.

questions.prompt: A single AppContext provides { user, theme, notifications, dispatch }. Profiling shows that every component that reads theme re-renders whenever notifications change. Explain why, and implement the correct fix.
successCriteria: Correctly identifies that the entire context value object is new on every state update, causing all consumers to re-render
Explains the value/dispatch separation pattern
Shows or describes splitting into at least two focused contexts: one for stable values, one for frequently updated values

rubric.excellent: Correctly traces the re-render cascade from reference equality, implements the split context pattern, and explains which consumers can now avoid unnecessary re-renders.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the single context as the problem and proposes splitting but does not fully implement or explain the value/dispatch separation.
rubric.weak: Proposes wrapping the context value in useMemo as the primary fix without addressing the structural problem.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes useMemo on the context value as a complete fix, missing that notification updates still invalidate the memo.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show that useMemo on the context value still creates a new reference when notifications change. Then walk through the split context: theme consumers never re-render for notification changes. Ask: what is the structural difference?

questions.prompt: A Modal component is rendered inside a parent div with transform: translateZ(0) applied for GPU compositing. The modal appears offset and ignores its z-index. What React feature resolves this and why?
successCriteria: Names React Portals (createPortal) as the solution
Explains that transform creates a new CSS stacking context that clips absolutely positioned descendants
States that a Portal renders the DOM output into document.body, outside the stacking context

rubric.excellent: Names Portals, correctly explains the stacking context mechanism, and shows or describes the createPortal call with document.body as the target.
rubric.adequate: Names Portals and identifies the stacking context issue but doesn't fully explain the mechanism.
rubric.weak: Proposes increasing z-index or using position: fixed without recognizing that the stacking context makes this ineffective.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes CSS-only fixes (z-index, position: fixed) without recognizing the stacking context problem that requires a Portal.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show a demo where z-index fails inside a transform stacking context. Then show the same modal rendered via a Portal into document.body behaving correctly. Ask: what did moving the DOM node solve that CSS could not?

questions.prompt: A useDropdown hook returns { isOpen, setIsOpen, activeIndex, setActiveIndex }. Apply the hook contract test. What is wrong, and what should the hook return instead?
successCriteria: Identifies that returning raw setters (setIsOpen, setActiveIndex) leaks internal state management
Notes that consumers can put the hook in invalid states by calling setters directly
Proposes returning prop getters (getTriggerProps, getMenuProps) and named action functions (open, close, selectItem) instead

rubric.excellent: Applies the contract test, identifies the encapsulation failure, and proposes prop getters and named actions as the correct interface.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the setter exposure as problematic but does not propose the prop getter pattern as the solution.
rubric.weak: Accepts the hook interface as valid because it provides access to all necessary state.

errorPatterns.description: Learner equates API completeness (all state accessible) with correct contract design, missing the encapsulation requirement.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show a consumer that calls setActiveIndex(-1) — an invalid state the hook doesn't handle. Show what breaks. Then show the same attempt via a selectItem(index) action that bounds-checks before updating. Ask: which design prevents the invalid state?

questions.prompt: A client needs a Select component that works as a native-styled dropdown on desktop and a bottom sheet on mobile — identical behavior, completely different presentation. Which pattern best fits this requirement, and why does a standard props-based approach fail?
successCriteria: Recommends a headless hook pattern (behavior-only hook with prop getters)
Explains that the headless approach separates behavior (open/close, keyboard nav, ARIA) from presentation
Identifies that a standard props-based component bakes in the markup, preventing completely different visual implementations

rubric.excellent: Recommends headless hook with prop getters, correctly explains the separation of concerns, and identifies what the props-based approach can't accommodate (different DOM structure for each presentation).
rubric.adequate: Recommends a headless approach but is imprecise about why the props-based approach fails.
rubric.weak: Recommends a compound component, missing that compound components still impose a DOM structure on consumers.

errorPatterns.description: Learner recommends compound components for the 'same behavior, different presentation' requirement, missing that compound components still control rendering structure.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show a compound component's Tabs.List and Tabs.Tab: these still render specific DOM elements. Ask: can a mobile consumer render a SwipeableDrawer instead of a Tab? Show that the headless hook allows any DOM structure while sharing behavior.

questions.prompt: A SaaS onboarding form has 15 fields. The developer uses fully controlled inputs with useState for each field. A designer reports that typing in the company name field feels slightly laggy on a mid-range laptop. Explain the root cause and propose a fix.
successCriteria: Identifies per-keystroke re-renders of all 15 fields as the root cause
Explains that each onChange call triggers a useState update, re-rendering the entire form
Recommends React Hook Form with register for uncontrolled inputs as the fix

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies per-keystroke re-renders, explains the controlled input mechanism, and proposes RHF with specific mention of the register pattern.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the re-render problem and mentions RHF but does not explain the register (uncontrolled) mechanism.
rubric.weak: Proposes debouncing setState or splitting into multiple components without addressing the controlled vs. uncontrolled architecture.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes debouncing or component splitting rather than switching to an uncontrolled approach.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show React DevTools highlight view on a 15-field controlled form. Count highlights per keystroke. Then show the same form with RHF register. Count highlights per keystroke. Ask: what changed, and why?

questions.prompt: A developer defines a TypeScript interface: interface InvoiceFormData { amount: number; dueDate: string; }. Separately, they have a Zod schema with the same shape. What is the architectural problem with maintaining both? Show the correct approach.
successCriteria: Identifies that the interface and schema can drift — the interface changes but the schema doesn't (or vice versa)
Shows z.infer<typeof schema> as the way to derive the TypeScript type from the Zod schema
Explains that this makes the schema the single source of truth for both runtime validation and compile-time types

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies the drift risk, shows z.infer syntax, and explains the single source of truth principle with its implications for maintenance.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the drift risk and mentions z.infer but doesn't fully articulate why this matters in a team context.
rubric.weak: Accepts maintaining both as valid, suggesting tests can catch drift.

errorPatterns.description: Learner accepts maintaining interface and schema separately, relying on tests to catch drift.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show a realistic scenario: a field is removed from the interface but not the schema. The TypeScript type says the field doesn't exist; the runtime schema still validates it. Show how the bug appears in production. Ask: what would z.infer have done differently?

questions.prompt: A server returns { error: 'postcode_invalid', field: 'postcode' } after form submission. The current code shows this as a toast notification and resets the entire form. Design the correct error handling architecture using React Hook Form.
successCriteria: Uses setError('postcode', { type: 'server', message: '...' }) to route the error back into the form's field state
Does not reset the form — all other field values are preserved
Displays the error adjacent to the postcode field using errors.postcode.message

rubric.excellent: Correctly uses setError, preserves all other field values, and places the error display inline at the field level with correct reasoning.
rubric.adequate: Uses setError correctly but does not explicitly address form preservation or field-level display.
rubric.weak: Proposes resetting the form, or displaying the error only as a toast without routing it to the field.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes resetting the form to show the error, not recognizing that setError can route server errors into RHF's field error state without affecting other fields.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show setError in action: call it, then log formState.values — all other fields are unchanged. Show errors.postcode.message displayed inline. Ask: what did the user experience before and after this fix?

questions.prompt: A bundle analysis shows pdfjs-dist (820kb) in the main application chunk. It is used only in a PDF export feature accessed by 3% of users. What is the architectural fix, and which users benefit?
successCriteria: Proposes component-level code splitting with React.lazy for the PDF export component
States that pdfjs-dist moves into a separate chunk loaded on demand
Identifies that 97% of users no longer pay the 820kb cost on initial load

rubric.excellent: Proposes React.lazy with dynamic import, shows or describes the Suspense boundary, and correctly quantifies the user benefit.
rubric.adequate: Proposes code splitting with correct intent but is imprecise about the React.lazy + Suspense integration.
rubric.weak: Proposes replacing pdfjs-dist with a smaller library without recognizing that on-demand loading is the architectural lever.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes library replacement as the primary solution rather than deferred loading via code splitting.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show the bundle size impact: even replacing pdfjs-dist with a 200kb alternative still ships 200kb to 97% of users who never use the feature. Then show lazy loading: 0kb shipped to those users. Ask: which is the architectural lever?

questions.prompt: A component renders a list of 12 comments per post. A developer proposes adding TanStack Virtual for performance. Evaluate this proposal and state when you would and would not apply virtualization.
successCriteria: Recommends against virtualization for 12 items — DOM cost is negligible
States virtualization is appropriate for lists of 200+ simultaneously rendered items
Notes virtualization complexity costs: scroll management, variable height measurement, accessibility limitations

rubric.excellent: Correctly rejects the proposal for 12 items with quantified reasoning, states the appropriate threshold, and articulates the complexity trade-offs.
rubric.adequate: Correctly rejects the proposal for 12 items but is imprecise about the threshold or trade-offs.
rubric.weak: Accepts virtualization as always beneficial regardless of list size.

errorPatterns.description: Learner accepts virtualization as universally beneficial regardless of list size.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show profiler output for a 12-item list: render time is sub-millisecond. Show TanStack Virtual implementation complexity for a variable-height list. Ask: does the complexity earn its cost for 12 items? At what number does the trade-off flip?

questions.prompt: A team enables the React Compiler on their codebase. A developer removes all useMemo and useCallback calls because 'the compiler handles it now'. What could go wrong? Identify two scenarios where manual memoization remains necessary.
successCriteria: Identifies that the compiler silently skips components it can't statically analyze
Names at least two valid remaining cases: genuinely expensive computations (>10ms), stable references for non-compiled third-party components, or stable context values
Notes the need to verify compiler coverage before removing manual memoization

rubric.excellent: Identifies the compiler's silent skipping behavior, names two correct remaining valid cases, and recommends verifying compiled output before removing manual memoizations.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the compiler limitation and names one valid remaining case.
rubric.weak: Accepts the developer's reasoning as correct, or believes the compiler guarantees complete coverage.

errorPatterns.description: Learner believes the React Compiler guarantees complete memoization coverage, removing all manual useMemo/useCallback.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show compiler debug output for a component with dynamic property access — the compiler skips it. Then show a profiler trace where that component re-renders expensively without the removed useMemo. Ask: what verification step was skipped?

questions.prompt: A 5-person team is building a SaaS notification center (simple queue: add, dismiss, mark-read). A senior developer recommends Redux Toolkit. Evaluate this recommendation against Zustand for this specific context.
successCriteria: Identifies the notification center as simple client state — no middleware, no audit log, no event sourcing
Recommends Zustand for this context: lower boilerplate, faster development, adequate for the feature
Notes RTK would be appropriate if the feature needed middleware, time-travel debugging, or event-sourced patterns
States a concrete review trigger for when to reconsider

rubric.excellent: Recommends Zustand with explicit reasoning tied to the feature's state characteristics, acknowledges RTK's valid use cases, and states a review trigger.
rubric.adequate: Recommends Zustand correctly but does not clearly articulate the state characteristics that drive the choice.
rubric.weak: Defers to RTK because it's 'the industry standard' without analyzing the feature's actual state needs.

errorPatterns.description: Learner defers to RTK as the safer or more professional choice without analyzing the feature's state characteristics.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: List the characteristics of the notification center: no middleware needed, no audit log, no normalized entities, 5-person team. Ask learner to match each RTK feature to a corresponding notification center requirement. How many match? Then do the same for Zustand.

questions.prompt: A developer stores all server-fetched data in Zustand. Three months later they want TanStack Query. List three things TanStack Query provides that Zustand does not, and explain why each matters for server state.
successCriteria: Names at least three of: automatic background refetch, staleness detection with staleTime, cache invalidation with invalidateQueries, request deduplication, optimistic update with rollback support
For each, explains the user-facing or maintenance consequence

rubric.excellent: Names three correct features, explains the user-facing consequence of each (e.g. stale data, wasted API calls, inconsistent UI after mutation), and articulates what manual code Zustand would require.
rubric.adequate: Names three correct features with partial explanations.
rubric.weak: Names features without explaining why they matter, or confuses TanStack Query features with Zustand capabilities.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats TanStack Query as a replacement for Zustand rather than a complement for a different state kind.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show the server state lifecycle properties table. For each property, show the Zustand code required to implement it manually. Then show TanStack Query's built-in equivalent. Ask: for client-only state (sidebar open), what is TanStack Query's queryFn? (There isn't one.)

questions.prompt: A client asks: 'Should we use Next.js or Vite for our internal SaaS tool? The tool is used only by logged-in employees — no public pages, no SEO requirements.' Make and defend a recommendation with one explicit condition that would change your answer.
successCriteria: Recommends Vite for an internal, authenticated-only SPA with no SEO requirements
Justifies with: simpler config, faster dev experience, no SSR complexity introduced unnecessarily
States a condition that would change the answer (e.g. if the app later needs public-facing pages, server-rendered reports, or edge-computed personalisation)

rubric.excellent: Recommends Vite with clear, requirements-driven reasoning and states a precise, realistic condition for reconsideration.
rubric.adequate: Recommends Vite correctly but is vague about the condition that would change the answer.
rubric.weak: Recommends Next.js 'just in case' of future SSR needs, without analyzing the actual requirements.

errorPatterns.description: Learner recommends Next.js as the safer default to cover future options, without recognizing the architectural constraints it introduces upfront.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: List Next.js's architectural constraints: file-based routing, server/client component boundary decisions, deployment model requirements. Ask: which of these constraints does this internal tool need today? Calculate the cost of importing these constraints 'just in case'.

questions.prompt: A test suite has 95% code coverage composed mostly of snapshot tests. A developer refactors a button from <button> to a custom <IconButton> component. 15 snapshot tests fail. After updating snapshots, the suite is green. Two weeks later, the button's onClick handler is accidentally removed. No test catches this. Explain both phenomena.
successCriteria: Explains that snapshot tests verify DOM structure, not behavior — refactoring structure breaks snapshots even when behavior is correct
Explains that a missing onClick handler is a behavioral regression not captured by snapshot tests
Recommends RTL behavioral tests that verify the user can click the button and trigger the expected outcome

rubric.excellent: Correctly diagnoses both phenomena (structure vs. behavior testing) and recommends a behavioral test approach with a concrete example.
rubric.adequate: Correctly diagnoses both phenomena but is vague about the recommended alternative.
rubric.weak: Accepts high coverage as equivalent to test quality, or blames the refactor rather than the test design.

errorPatterns.description: Learner equates high coverage with test quality, or blames the developer for the refactor breaking tests.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show the snapshot test and the onClick removal side by side. Ask: which test catches the snapshot breakage? Which catches the onClick removal? Then show an RTL test: await user.click(button); expect(handler).toHaveBeenCalled(). Ask: which regression does this catch that the snapshot missed?

questions.prompt: A test mocks axios.get to return fixture data for a component that uses TanStack Query internally. The team migrates from Axios to the native Fetch API. All these tests break despite the component's behavior being unchanged. What is the root cause, and what test setup would have survived the migration?
successCriteria: Identifies that mocking axios.get couples the test to the HTTP client implementation
Explains that MSW intercepts at the network level — the actual fetch/axios call happens, but the response is mocked at the network boundary
States that MSW tests survive an Axios → Fetch migration because the URL and response shape are mocked, not the client

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies the coupling to the HTTP client, explains MSW's network-level interception, and states the specific reason MSW tests survive HTTP client refactors.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the HTTP client coupling and mentions MSW but is imprecise about why MSW is at a different boundary.
rubric.weak: Proposes mocking fetch instead of Axios as the fix, missing that the problem is the level of mocking, not the choice of client.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes switching from mocking Axios to mocking fetch, missing that both approaches couple tests to the HTTP client implementation.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Draw the stack: Component → TanStack Query → Axios/Fetch → Network. Show where axios.get mock intercepts vs. where MSW intercepts. Ask: if you swap Axios for Fetch, which mock is invalidated? Show that MSW sits below both.

questions.prompt: Design a testing strategy for a SaaS billing feature with: an invoice list (paginated, server data), a payment form (Zod validation, mutation), and a 'cancel subscription' flow (revenue-critical, multi-step confirmation). Specify: which layer (unit/integration/E2E) covers each concern, and what specific tests you would write first.
successCriteria: Integration tests for invoice list: happy path load, error state, pagination
Unit test for Zod schema: edge cases (invalid card, expired date); integration test for form submission happy path and server error routing
E2E test for cancel subscription: full multi-step flow because it is revenue-critical
Prioritises integration tests as the majority layer, E2E only for critical flows

rubric.excellent: Correctly assigns all three features to appropriate test layers with specific test descriptions, and correctly identifies cancel subscription as the E2E candidate based on revenue criticality.
rubric.adequate: Correctly assigns most features to appropriate layers but is vague about the specific tests or misassigns one feature.
rubric.weak: Uses unit tests for component behavior or snapshots as the primary strategy.

errorPatterns.description: Learner uses unit tests with mocked components as the primary testing strategy, or treats E2E as the correct layer for all three features.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Apply the value-to-cost calibration: show the maintenance cost of a heavily mocked unit test suite for a component vs. an RTL integration test. Show the E2E cost for a non-critical feature. Ask: which layer gives the best return for each concern?

questions.prompt: An existing SaaS billing module stores all server data in Redux, has a single root Error Boundary, and uses snapshot tests only. Produce an audit listing three liabilities in priority order. For each: name it, state the impact, and prescribe the remediation.
successCriteria: Lists three distinct liabilities across different audit lenses (not three state problems)
Assigns correct priority order — the single root Error Boundary ranks highest as an immediate user-facing risk
Each liability includes: name, measurable impact, and specific remediation action

rubric.excellent: Lists three liabilities across distinct lenses, correctly prioritises by impact, and provides specific remediations with concrete actions (not vague suggestions).
rubric.adequate: Lists three liabilities but prioritises incorrectly or provides vague remediations.
rubric.weak: Lists fewer than three, focuses only on one lens (e.g. state management), or provides style opinions rather than architectural liabilities.

errorPatterns.description: Learner lists style opinions (component is too long, variable names are unclear) as architectural liabilities.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the liability definition: an architectural liability has a measurable impact. Have learner reclassify each finding as 'causes bug class', 'causes performance cost', 'blocks feature development', or 'style opinion'. Only the first three are liabilities.

questions.prompt: A product brief describes a new team workload feature: a grid of team members with task assignments, drag-and-drop reassignment, filter bar, overloaded warning, and a manager settings panel. Produce a state topology map and async boundary map for this feature.
successCriteria: State topology correctly classifies: team/task data as server cache, filter state as local UI or URL param, drag state as ephemeral, settings form as form state, overloaded warning as derived
Async boundary map shows independent boundaries for the grid and settings panel
Error boundaries are scoped to the feature, not just the app root
No server state placed in a global store

rubric.excellent: Produces a complete topology map with correct classifications and mechanisms, and a boundary map with appropriate granularity and error boundary scoping.
rubric.adequate: Produces mostly correct topology map with one misclassification. Boundary map is present but granularity justification is incomplete.
rubric.weak: Puts all state in a global store, or produces a boundary map with only a root-level boundary.
