errorPatterns.description: Learner produces an architecture that is technically sophisticated (RTK + Saga + normalized entity adapter) but inappropriate for a 4-person team with a 6-week timeline.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Have learner estimate: how many hours to implement the proposed architecture vs. Zustand + TanStack Query? What does the team gain from the additional complexity given the feature requirements? Apply the SMB SaaS carrying cost principle.

questions.prompt: A product owner asks: 'Our developers want to migrate from storing API data in Redux to something called TanStack Query. It will take a sprint. Should we approve it?' Write a 3-sentence business-language response.
successCriteria: Avoids technical jargon (no mention of 'server cache', 'staleTime', 'queryKey')
States a concrete business benefit (users see fresh data automatically, support tickets decrease, specific feature becomes cheaper to build)
States the cost (one sprint) and the consequence of not doing it (ongoing maintenance cost, class of bugs)

rubric.excellent: Provides a business-language response with concrete benefit, cost, and consequence of inaction — all in terms a product owner can act on.
rubric.adequate: Provides mostly business language but includes one or two technical terms that require explanation.
rubric.weak: Provides a technical explanation of TanStack Query's features without translating to business impact.

errorPatterns.description: Learner uses technical terms (caching, staleness, invalidation) without translating them to business consequences.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the business translation table. For each technical term in the learner's response, ask: what does this mean in terms of what the product owner cares about: user experience, team speed, bug rate, or cost? Have learner rewrite with those terms.

glossary.definition: React's internal unit of work. Each component instance corresponds to a fiber node storing the component's type, props, state, and output from its last render. The fiber tree is what React works with during reconciliation.
The process by which React compares the output of the current render to the previous fiber tree and determines the minimal set of DOM mutations required. Also called diffing.

The phase where React applies mutations determined during reconciliation to the actual DOM. Side effects (useEffect, useLayoutEffect) are scheduled and flushed here. This phase is synchronous and cannot be interrupted.
The scheduler's mechanism for processing fiber units of work. In concurrent mode, the work loop can be interrupted between fibers to yield to higher-priority work.
React's optimization where it skips re-rendering a subtree because props and state haven't changed. React.memo, PureComponent, and shouldComponentUpdate all trigger bailouts.
React 18's internal priority model. Each update is assigned a lane (e.g. SyncLane for urgent updates, TransitionLane for non-urgent). The scheduler processes higher-priority lanes first.
The React 18+ rendering model where the work loop can be paused and resumed, allowing React to keep the UI responsive by yielding to urgent updates mid-render.
A <Suspense> component that catches 'suspended' renders from its subtree and displays a fallback prop until the suspended work resolves.
A component that, during rendering, throws a Promise. React catches the thrown promise, renders the nearest Suspense boundary's fallback, and retries the component when the promise resolves.
A class component implementing componentDidCatch and/or getDerivedStateFromError, catching JavaScript errors thrown during rendering in its subtree.
The architectural decision of how large or small a subtree each Suspense or Error Boundary covers. Fine granularity allows regions to fail or load independently.

A React 18 hook returning [isPending, startTransition]. Wrapping a state update in startTransition marks it as non-urgent, allowing React to keep the current UI interactive while preparing the next state.

A React 18 hook that accepts a value and returns a deferred version. React uses the old value for rendering until the main thread is free. Unlike useTransition, it defers a value rather than wrapping a setter call.
A React 19 hook that can consume both Promises and Context values inside a component. When passed a Promise, it integrates with Suspense. Unlike other hooks, use() can be called conditionally.

A sequential loading pattern where component B can't start loading until component A has finished, creating cumulative latency. Proper Suspense boundary design exposes waterfalls and enables parallel data fetching.

A union type where each member has a shared literal field (the discriminant) that TypeScript uses to narrow to the correct member. Example: type State = { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'success'; data: User } | { status: 'error'; error: Error }.

A technique for creating nominal typing in TypeScript's structural type system. A UserId and an OrderId that are both string at runtime become incompatible at the type level by attaching a phantom brand.
The process by which TypeScript refines a broad type to a specific member within a conditional block. Used to safely access fields that only exist on specific union members.
A bound on a type parameter limiting what types it can be. <T extends { id: string }> requires that T has an id field.
A pattern using a never-typed default branch to ensure a switch or if-chain handles every member of a union. If a new member is added, the compiler flags the uncovered branch.
A function that always returns the same output for the same inputs and has no side effects. React component render functions must be pure.

Any interaction with the outside world from within a function: mutating external state, making network requests, reading from a DOM, writing to a log. React isolates side effects to useEffect and event handlers.
The principle that data is never mutated in place. Updates produce new values; old values remain unchanged. This makes change detection cheap (reference equality) and enables safe memoization.
An optimization where an immutable update reuses unchanged parts of a data structure rather than copying the whole thing. Used by Immer and Zustand.
A function that takes a function as an argument or returns a function. Array.map, Array.filter, and React's HOC pattern are all higher-order functions.
Combining two or more functions so the output of one becomes the input of the next. Manifests in React at the component, hook, and selector levels.
A property of pure functions: any call can be replaced with its return value without changing program behavior. This is what makes memoization safe.
The map of what state exists in an application, what kind each piece is, and which layer owns it.
Data that originates on the server and is cached client-side. Has unique lifecycle properties: staleness, background refetch, cache invalidation. Examples: user records, paginated lists.
Client-owned state that multiple unrelated parts of the UI need simultaneously. Examples: current user session, theme preference, notification queue.
State relevant only to one component or a small local subtree. Examples: whether a dropdown is open, a hover state, a local toggle.
State computed from other state. Should never be stored independently — recompute or memoize it. Examples: filtered lists, totals, formatted values.
Transient state with a very short lifetime, typically tied to a single interaction. Examples: tooltip visibility, drag position, animation state.
Placing state as close as possible to the components that use it. The first principle of state ownership: lift only as high as necessary.
Passing props through intermediate components that don't use them, solely to deliver data to a deeply nested consumer. A symptom of incorrect state placement.
A set of components that share implicit state via Context, designed to be composed by the consumer. The consumer controls layout; the parent controls state.
A component or hook that provides behavior and state with no opinions about rendering. The consumer owns all markup and styles.
A function that takes a component and returns a new component with additional behavior injected. Example: withAuth(Dashboard).
A pattern where a component receives a function as a prop and calls it to produce its output, giving the consumer control over rendering.
Separating a component's data-fetching and logic (container) from its pure rendering (presentational). In modern React, the container is typically a custom hook.
A React feature that renders a component's output into a different DOM node than its parent. Used for modals, tooltips, and toasts that must escape CSS stacking contexts.
A CSS concept where a positioned element creates an isolated rendering layer. transform, opacity < 1, and z-index on positioned elements all create new stacking contexts.
The public interface of a custom hook: what it accepts, what it returns, and what lifecycle guarantees it makes — independent of its implementation.
An input whose value is driven by React state. Every keystroke calls setState, causing a re-render. The component is the source of truth.
An input that manages its own value in the DOM. React reads the value via a ref only when needed (e.g. on submit). The DOM is the source of truth.
A form library using uncontrolled inputs via refs. Avoids per-keystroke re-renders. Provides register, handleSubmit, formState, and watch.
A TypeScript-first schema validation library. A Zod schema is both a runtime validator and a TypeScript type source. z.infer<typeof schema> extracts the static type.
RHF's integration point with validation libraries. zodResolver(schema) wires a Zod schema into RHF's validation pipeline.
The async lifecycle of a write operation: idle → pending → success | error. Distinct from form input state.
Updating UI state immediately on user action before the server confirms, then reconciling with the server response. Reduces perceived latency.
Dividing an application's JavaScript bundle into smaller chunks that load on demand rather than upfront. Reduces initial parse and execution time.
import('./Module') — a JavaScript expression (not a static declaration) that loads a module asynchronously, returning a Promise. The mechanism behind React.lazy.
The bundler's removal of exported code that is never imported. Requires ES module syntax; CommonJS (require) is not tree-shakeable.
Inspecting the contents and sizes of production chunks to identify what is large, duplicated, or should be split or deferred.
Rendering only the DOM nodes currently visible in a scrollable list, rather than all items. Critical for lists of hundreds or thousands of items.
A build-time compiler that statically analyses components and hooks and automatically inserts memoization where safe. Stable in React 19.
The official, opinionated Redux wrapper. Provides createSlice, createAsyncThunk, createSelector, and RTK Query. Reduces Redux boilerplate while preserving its middleware ecosystem.
A minimal global state library. A store is a hook. No reducers, no actions, no providers required. Best suited for simple cross-cutting client UI state.
RTK/Reselect's memoized selector factory. Computes derived state from store slices; re-runs only when inputs change.
A server cache management library managing fetching, caching, background refetching, and invalidation for server state. Not a replacement for client state managers.
TanStack Query's cache address. An array that uniquely identifies a query. Cache invalidation, refetching, and deduplication all key off this.
TanStack Query's hook for write operations. Manages the idle → pending → success | error lifecycle with onSuccess/onError callbacks.
A data-fetching function colocated with a route definition, executing before the route renders. Enables data and component to arrive together.
A build tool using native ES modules in development and Rollup for production. Fast HMR, minimal config. The default choice for new React SPAs without SSR requirements.
A testing shape that prioritizes integration tests over unit tests for UI code, with a small layer of E2E and a thin base of unit tests for pure logic.
A testing utility that renders components into a real DOM and provides queries mirroring how users find elements: by role, label, text — not by CSS class or component name.
A library that intercepts HTTP requests at the service worker or Node.js level, returning mock responses. Tests run against real fetch/Axios code — only the network is mocked.
An RTL utility that mounts a hook in a minimal wrapper component, allowing hooks to be called and tested in isolation without building a full component.
RTL's companion library for simulating user interactions (typing, clicking, tabbing) more accurately than fireEvent. Dispatches realistic browser events in sequence.
A test that verifies a module's public interface rather than its implementation. Survives internal refactoring; breaks only when the contract changes.
A structured review of an existing codebase identifying architectural liabilities: patterns that increase maintenance cost, introduce bugs, or block future feature development.
A specific, demonstrable problem in the current architecture with a measurable impact — a bug class it creates, a performance cost, or a feature it blocks. Not a style opinion.
A pre-implementation design document covering state topology, component model, async boundary placement, library selections, and testing strategy for a feature.

The ranking of liabilities by: severity of impact × cost to fix × risk of not fixing. High = causes user-facing bugs or blocks features. Medium = increases maintenance cost. Low = violates best practice with no current measurable impact.
An architectural decision expressed in terms a non-technical stakeholder can act on: time-to-market, maintenance cost, team scalability, reliability, and risk — not implementation details.
Architectural decisions that were expedient at the time but accumulate interest: each subsequent change costs more because of them.

A function returned from a headless hook that produces a pre-built object of props (event handlers, ARIA attributes) to spread onto a consumer's element. Encapsulates the hook's behavioral requirements without dictating the element type.
